skill.name: generate-api-client
skill.description: Generates a type-safe Orval API client from an OpenAPI specification.
skill.summary: Create generated TypeScript API clients with Orval from api-docs.yaml.
skill.author: Crearize

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported nine external command patterns and one weak cryptography pattern. The weak cryptography alert is a false positive on the description line, and the command alerts are markdown workflow examples with fixed commands. The remaining risk is low because the workflow asks an agent to run project package scripts and local toolchain commands.

low_findings.title: External Command Workflow Requires Trusted Repository Context
low_findings.description: The static command findings are markdown workflow examples, not hidden shell execution. The commands are fixed and do not interpolate user input, but pnpm and npx can execute project-defined tooling in the current repository.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file clearly instructs use of ls, cd, pnpm, and npx in fenced markdown examples. There is no evidence of dynamic shell construction, credential access, or exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Dismissed
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alert points to the YAML description line. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing routine, encryption routine, secret handling, or credential storage appears in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is a plain language skill description. I found no evidence of weak cryptographic code or crypto-related behavior in SKILL.md.

dangerous_patterns.title: Package Script and Local Tool Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow directs the agent to run pnpm run generate:api and npx tsc --noEmit. These are legitimate development commands, but they execute repository-controlled dependencies and scripts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command text is explicit and fixed in the skill instructions. The risk depends on the repository's package scripts and installed dependencies, not on malicious logic in the skill itself.

content.value_statement: Manual API client updates are slow and easy to break after backend schema changes. This skill guides Claude, Codex, or Claude Code through Orval generation and TypeScript validation.
actual_capabilities: Checks for the expected OpenAPI file at backend/src/main/resources/static/api-docs.yaml.
Runs the frontend Orval generation script with pnpm.
Checks the generated API client output directory.
Runs TypeScript validation with npx tsc --noEmit.
Produces a concise completion report with generated file paths.
limitations: Requires an existing frontend project with a generate:api script.
Assumes the OpenAPI specification path is backend/src/main/resources/static/api-docs.yaml.
Does not create Orval configuration from scratch.
Does not verify API behavior against a live backend.

use_cases.title: Refresh Clients After Backend Changes
use_cases.description: Regenerate the frontend API client after api-docs.yaml changes and confirm TypeScript still passes.

use_cases.title: Add API Generation to Pull Requests
use_cases.description: Run the documented generation flow before submitting a pull request that changes API contracts.

use_cases.title: Validate Generated Client Artifacts
use_cases.description: Confirm that expected generated files exist and that the TypeScript project accepts them.

prompt_templates.title: Generate the API Client
prompt_templates.scenario: Basic regeneration from the existing OpenAPI file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the generate-api-client skill to regenerate the Orval API client from the current api-docs.yaml file.

prompt_templates.title: Regenerate and Check Types
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the API client generation workflow, verify the generated files, and run the TypeScript type check.

prompt_templates.title: Review Generation Results
prompt_templates.scenario: Inspect generated outputs after a backend contract change.
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate the API client, summarize which generated files changed, and report any TypeScript errors.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare API Client Update for Review
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced workflow before a pull request.
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the full Orval generation flow, validate TypeScript, identify generated API artifacts, and prepare a concise completion report.

output_examples.input: Generate the API client from the latest OpenAPI specification.
output_examples.output: The assistant checks that api-docs.yaml exists, runs the Orval generation script, verifies generated files, and reports completion.

output_examples.input: Regenerate the client and confirm the frontend still type-checks.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs generation, checks the output directory, runs TypeScript validation, and lists any blocking errors.

output_examples.input: Prepare a short report after API client generation.
output: TypeScript validation passed or errors were reported.

best_practices: Run the skill only in repositories you trust because package scripts can execute local project code.
Review generated API changes before committing them.
Regenerate the client whenever the OpenAPI specification changes.
anti_patterns: Do not run generation when api-docs.yaml is missing or stale.
Do not ignore TypeScript errors after generation.
Do not hand-edit generated client files unless the project explicitly supports it.

faq.question: What tool does this skill use for generation?
faq.answer: It uses the repository's Orval generation script through pnpm run generate:api.

faq.question: Which OpenAPI file path does it expect?
Does it create a new Orval configuration?
faq.answer: No. It assumes the project already has the needed Orval setup and package script.

faq.question: Does it validate the generated TypeScript?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow runs npx tsc --noEmit after generation.

faq.question: Can it be used with Claude Code and Codex?
faq.answer: Yes. The report marks support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: What is the main security caution?
faq.answer: The workflow runs package commands, so use it only in trusted repositories.
